summary: Moved Ness Yo-Yo motion variable fields out of an inline nested struct inside union ftNess_MotionVars and into a separately declared struct ftNess_YoyoVars, then used that type for the attackhi4 and attacklw4 union members. The stated motivation was that the permuter reported a "double defined struct" when running permuter.sh; the change preserves the same fields and comments while avoiding the duplicate inline struct definition pattern.
what_changed: Added a standalone struct ftNess_YoyoVars in src/melee/ft/chara/ftNess/types.h.
Removed the inline nested declaration of struct ftNess_YoyoVars from inside union ftNess_MotionVars.

Changed the ftNess_MotionVars union members from a single inline struct declaration with two declarators, attackhi4 and attacklw4, to two explicit members: struct ftNess_YoyoVars attackhi4 and struct ftNess_YoyoVars attacklw4.
Kept the Yo-Yo variable layout and documented fields unchanged: yoyoCurrentFrame at 0x2340, yoyoRehitTimer at 0x2344, isChargeDisable at 0x2348, and isPosUpdateMod at 0x234C.
smart_moves: Solved a permuter-facing type-definition issue without changing the semantic contents or documented offsets of the Ness Yo-Yo motion variables.
Reused one named struct type for both attackhi4 and attacklw4, which makes the shared layout explicit and avoids repeating a nested struct declaration.
Kept the refactor narrowly scoped to ftNess/types.h, reducing risk for a change whose purpose was tooling compatibility rather than behavior.
decomp_lessons: If the permuter complains about a "double defined struct", check for inline named struct declarations embedded in unions or structs, especially when the same named type is declared alongside multiple members.
For fighter motion vars shared by multiple action states, prefer a separately declared named struct and then reference it in the motion union.
Header-only struct organization can affect decomp tooling even when the C layout and generated behavior are intended to remain unchanged.
When refactoring type declarations for tooling, preserve field order, documented offsets, names, and comments to make clear that the layout is unchanged.
naming_conventions: Ness-specific fighter variable types use the ftNess_ prefix, as in ftNess_FighterVars, ftNess_MotionVars, ftNess_YoyoVars, and ftNess_SpecialNVars.
Motion variable union members are named after action/state concepts such as attackhi4 and attacklw4.
Yo-Yo variable fields use descriptive camelCase names: yoyoCurrentFrame, yoyoRehitTimer, isChargeDisable, and isPosUpdateMod.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: This PR was motivated by permuter.sh/tooling behavior rather than by an assembly matching change visible in the excerpt.
Separating a named struct declaration from union member declarations is a low-risk matching tactic when field layout is preserved but C tooling dislikes the inline definition form.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided slice.
The author noted they believed the change should have no further impact but were not fully certain due to being new to C.
The PR body explicitly cited the permuter getting upset with a "double defined struct" when running permuter.sh as the reason for the refactor.
follow_up_queries: Find other fighter types.h files with inline named structs inside motion variable unions that may trigger permuter double-defined-struct issues.
Search for ft*_MotionVars unions that declare a named struct and multiple members in the same inline declaration.
Check whether other shared motion-var layouts can be split into standalone ftCharacter_*Vars structs without changing offsets.
